title: Job Search & Employment Services
description: Free statewide employment help — job search assistance, career counseling and workshops, skills matching (iMatchSkills), apprenticeships, veteran hiring services, and unemployment work-search support — at WorkSource centers across Oregon.
engage: Find your local WorkSource center at worksourceoregon.org/contact, or register at iMatchSkills.org. Access in person or by phone (11 languages supported).
content:
Services

  • Job search assistance and job postings
  • Career counseling and workshops
  • iMatchSkills skills matching
  • Apprenticeship programs
  • Veteran hiring services
  • Unemployment insurance work-search support

How to Access

Visit a WorkSource center (statewide), call your local office, or use the online My WorkSource portal.

title: ID & Document Replacement Assistance
description: A mutual-aid project that pays to replace lost identity documents — driver's licenses, ID cards, and birth certificates — by writing checks directly to the DMV or vital records offices. Free in-person clinics 3–4 times a month at rotating Portland locations. No appointment, no one turned away for lack of funds.
engage: Attend a free clinic (rotating locations — e.g. Blanchet House, William Temple House, Street Books). Check paulsusi.wordpress.com/pdx-id-assistance for current dates. No proof of income required.
content:
What It Covers

  • Driver's licenses and ID cards
  • Birth certificates

Who It's For

Anyone needing help replacing ID — aimed at people experiencing houselessness, but open to all regardless of income. No proof of poverty required.

How to Access

Attend a free in-person clinic held 3–4 times monthly at rotating Portland locations. No appointment necessary.

Note

Does not assist with REAL ID-compliant IDs; standard IDs meet most needs.

title: Temporary Pet Care During Treatment
description: Temporary foster care for the pets of people entering inpatient medical care or addiction treatment, so no one has to choose between getting help and keeping their animal. Community-based fostering model.
engage: Email fernsplaceinfo@gmail.com to ask about temporary pet care or fostering.
content:
What It Offers

Safe, temporary foster care for pets while their owners are in inpatient medical or addiction treatment.

Who It's For

People undergoing medical treatment or addiction recovery who need someone to care for their pet during their stay.
centerings: People with Pets/Animals; People in Recovery

title: Ticket to Work (Employment Support)
description: A free Social Security program helping people who receive SSDI or SSI disability benefits explore work and move toward financial independence — job coaching, placement, training, and guidance on how working affects your benefits, with Work Incentives that protect benefits and Medicare/Medicaid.
engage: Call the Ticket to Work Help Line at 1-866-968-7842 (TTY 1-866-833-2967), Mon–Fri 8am–8pm ET, email TicketToWork@ssa.gov, or use the Find Help tool at choosework.ssa.gov/findhelp.
content:
What It Offers

  • Employment support through authorized providers
  • Job coaching, placement, and training
  • Guidance on how work affects your benefits
  • Work Incentives that protect benefits as you try working

Who's Eligible

People receiving SSDI or SSI who want to work. Free and voluntary.

title: Coordinated Housing Access
description: The entry point for housing crisis assistance in Clackamas County — connecting residents experiencing or at risk of homelessness to shelter and housing resources through coordinated intake.
engage: Call Clackamas County Coordinated Housing Access at 503-655-8575 (Mon–Fri 8am–6pm).
content:
What It Does

Coordinated Housing Access (CHA) is the front door to housing and homelessness services in Clackamas County, screening residents and connecting them to shelter and housing programs.

Who It's For

Clackamas County residents experiencing or at risk of homelessness.

title: Primary & Whole-Person Health Care
description: Team-based primary medical care with integrated behavioral health for everyone in Multnomah County, including uninsured and underinsured people, across multiple clinics plus a mobile medical van.
engage: Call 503-988-5558 to become a patient or schedule, or use the MyChart portal. Multiple clinic locations countywide.
content:
Services

Primary and preventive medical care, integrated behavioral health, and a mobile clinic van serving multiple weekly locations. Everyone in Multnomah County is welcome, including uninsured and underinsured residents (federally-funded health center).

title: Dental Care
description: Dental services — routine check-ups, fillings, urgent care, and school-based dental programs — through Multnomah County's community health clinics, open to county residents including the uninsured.
engage: Call 503-988-5558 to schedule dental care at a Multnomah County clinic.
content:
Services

  • Routine check-ups and cleanings
  • Fillings and restorative care
  • Urgent dental care
  • School-based dental programs (ages 5–18)

Who It's For

Multnomah County residents, including uninsured and underinsured people.

title: HIV Care & Support
description: Comprehensive HIV medical care plus connections to housing and food assistance, for people living with HIV in Multnomah County.
engage: Call 503-988-5558 to access HIV care through Multnomah County's health center.
content:
Services

Comprehensive HIV treatment integrated with support connecting patients to housing and food assistance.

Who It's For

People living with HIV in Multnomah County.

title: 24/7 Behavioral Health Crisis Line
organization_name: Multnomah County Behavioral Health Call Center
description: A free, confidential 24/7 mental health crisis line staffed by trained clinicians — crisis counseling, referrals to community providers, mobile crisis dispatch, and help finding local behavioral health supports. Interpretation available, with a dedicated Spanish line.
engage: Call 503-988-4888 (English) or 503-988-4853 (Línea Latine / Spanish), 24/7. Free and confidential.
content:
What It Offers

  • Free, confidential 24/7 behavioral health crisis support
  • Referrals to affordable community agencies
  • Help finding local behavioral health providers
  • Mobile crisis response dispatch
  • Language interpretation

Who It's For

Anyone experiencing emotional distress, including people calling on behalf of someone else.

title: Harm Reduction & Bridges to Care (HRBR)
description: Low-barrier, on-demand medication for addiction treatment (opioids, alcohol, nicotine, meth, kratom) without requiring abstinence, plus peer support and HIV/hepatitis C screening and treatment. A 'bridge' clinic that stabilizes people and connects them to long-term care. Ages 15+.
engage: Call 503-494-2100 to schedule. Virtual clinic Mon–Fri 10am–7pm (must be in Oregon); in-person at 10101 SE Main St, Suite 1001, Portland, Mon–Fri 1–5pm. Uninsured patients referred to financial assistance.
content:
Services

  • Low-barrier, on-demand medication for addiction treatment (opioid, alcohol, nicotine, kratom, and methamphetamine use disorders)
  • Peer support services
  • HIV and hepatitis C screening and treatment
  • Limited behavioral health support

Who It's For

People ages 15 and older who use drugs — no abstinence required. Does not treat acute alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal.

title: CHAT Mobile Community Health Team
description: A mobile health team responding to non-emergency 9-1-1 calls with specialized medical care instead of fire trucks — treating minor injuries, overdoses, and other low-acuity health needs, with overdose response and 24-hour aftercare follow-up connecting people to resources.
engage: Call 9-1-1 for a medical situation — dispatchers determine whether CHAT is the right response. General info: 311.
content:
What It Does

Portland Fire & Rescue's Community Health Assess and Treat (CHAT) program sends medical teams to non-urgent 9-1-1 calls, including an Overdose Response Team (Downtown, Old Town, SE Portland) and Aftercare Teams that follow up within 24 hours to connect people to healthcare and resources.

Who It's For

People with non-emergency health concerns, especially vulnerable and socially isolated individuals.

title: Children's Clothes Closet
description: Free children's clothing (sizes preemie through age 17), maternity wear, children's shoes, and baby supplies for families in need, at St. Mark's Lutheran Church in SE Portland. By appointment.
engage: Schedule an appointment via the signup link at smpdx.org/clothes, or call 503-777-1443. Located at St. Mark's Lutheran Church, 5415 SE Powell Blvd, Portland.
content:
What It Offers

  • Free children's clothing, sizes preemie–17
  • Maternity wear
  • Children's shoes and baby supplies

Who It's For

Families needing children's clothing — open to anyone in need.

How to Access

By appointment (schedule online). Located on the main floor, accessible entrance near the parking lot.

title: Family Transitional Housing
description: Christ-centered transitional housing in Gresham for families with children experiencing homelessness — 6-month and 1-year residential placements with stability, structure, and support. Includes a food pantry, clothing assistance, weekly case management, and an on-site Children's Center.
engage: Call (503) 492-3046 or email info@familyshelter.org. Located at 5003 W Powell Blvd, Gresham.
content:
Services

  • 6-month and 1-year residential family housing
  • Food pantry access
  • Clothing assistance
  • Weekly case management and goal-setting
  • On-site Children's Center

Who It's For

Families with children experiencing homelessness, working toward long-term stability.

title: LifeChange for Women & Children
description: A free 12-month, Bible-based residential recovery program offering a safe, healing home for women and families to transform their lives through classes, group studies, and holistic support.
engage: Call 503-802-2271 or visit ugmportland.org/help-for-women to learn about LifeChange for Women & Children.
content:
Program

LifeChange is a free, 12-month, Bible-based residential program providing a safe, healing home for women and their children. Whole-person healing through classes, group studies, and community support.

Who It's For

Women and families seeking recovery and life transformation.

title: Free Meals (Founders Café)
description: Free, freshly-prepared meals served to anyone who comes to the door — breakfast, lunch, and dinner, Monday–Saturday, at Blanchet House's downtown Portland location. No registration or eligibility requirements. Clothing is also distributed to those in need.
engage: Come to 310 NW Glisan St, Portland during meal hours (Mon–Sat). No registration required. Call 503-241-4340 with questions.
content:
What's Offered

  • Free breakfast, lunch, and dinner, Monday–Saturday
  • Clothing for those in need

Who It's For

Anyone experiencing hunger, homelessness, or poverty — all are welcome, no questions asked.

title: Bethanie's Room Women's Overnight Shelter
description: A year-round overnight shelter for women 18 and older, open 8pm–8am every night, providing safe accommodations at Blanchet House's downtown location.
engage: Come to 310 NW Glisan St, Portland in the evening (open 8pm–8am nightly). Call 503-241-4340 with questions.
content:
Shelter

Bethanie's Room is a safe overnight shelter for women 18+, open 8pm to 8am, 365 days a year.

Who It's For

Women 18 and older needing safe overnight shelter.

title: Residential Recovery Program (House Program)
description: A free, eight-month residential program at Blanchet House's downtown location offering a safe space to recover, refocus, and build toward a stable future through a dignity-centered approach.
engage: Call 503-241-4340 to ask about the residential House Program.
content:
Program

An 8-month residential program providing a free, safe space to recover and rebuild, with support toward each resident's goals.

Who It's For

People working toward recovery and stability.

title: Blanchet Farm (Men's Residential Recovery)
description: A rural residential recovery program in Yamhill County for men addressing substance addiction through therapeutic work — gardening, animal care, beekeeping, carpentry — plus on-site 12-step meetings, case management, and peer support. Up to one year of free room and board.
engage: Call 503-852-6626 or 503-241-4340 x117 (Mon–Fri before 4pm) for a phone interview. Requires willingness to stay sober, ability to do farm work, and a background check. Located near Carlton, OR (about an hour from Portland).
content:
Program

  • Up to one year of free room and board
  • On-site 12-step meetings
  • Therapeutic work: gardening, animal care, beekeeping, carpentry
  • Case management and peer support from staff in recovery
  • Access to mental and physical health services

Who It's For

Men committed to sobriety who can perform moderate physical labor. Requires a phone interview, negative drug screen (except THC), and background check.

title: Gresham Women's Shelter
description: A year-round shelter for people identifying as women, nonbinary, or genderqueer experiencing homelessness. Domestic-violence-informed (though not a secure DV shelter). Access by referral list.
engage: Call 971-333-1223 with your contact information to get on the referral list. For DV situations, contact Gateway Center or Call to Safety.
content:
Shelter

Year-round shelter for women, nonbinary, and genderqueer individuals experiencing homelessness. DV-informed but not a secure domestic violence shelter.

How to Access

By referral — call 971-333-1223 to be added to the list.

title: Lilac Meadows Family Shelter
description: A year-round shelter for families (one or more adults with at least one minor child) or individuals in their third trimester of pregnancy. Access through coordinated intake via 211.
engage: Call 211 or visit 211info.org — 211 is the main way to access family shelter in Multnomah County. No walk-ins.
content:
Shelter

Year-round family shelter for households with at least one minor child, or individuals in their third trimester.

How to Access

Coordinated intake through 211info.org — no walk-in access.

title: Housing Assistance & Family Advocacy
description: One-time cash assistance, temporary support, and permanent supportive housing (with Multnomah County), plus person-centered family advocacy — employment support, debt help, disability access, and Social Security / Oregon Health Plan navigation.
engage: Call (503) 548-0200 to ask about housing assistance and family advocacy.
content:
Services

  • Housing assistance: one-time cash aid, temporary support, and permanent supportive housing
  • Family advocacy: employment support, debt help, disability access, SSI/OHP navigation

Who It's For

Families and individuals experiencing or at risk of homelessness in Multnomah County.

title: Emergency Shelter & Meals (Burnside)
description: Emergency shelter, warm meals, and immediate relief for people experiencing homelessness at Portland Rescue Mission's Burnside location — the first step in a Christ-centered path toward stability and recovery.
engage: Call 503-647-7466 (503-MISSION) or visit the Burnside shelter. See the 'Need Help' section at portlandrescuemission.org.
content:
Services

  • Emergency overnight shelter
  • Warm meals
  • Immediate relief and stabilization support
  • Connection to recovery and housing programs

Who It's For

People experiencing homelessness.

title: New Life Recovery Program
description: A Christ-centered residential recovery program with counseling, stabilization, job training, and housing support. 84% of graduates from the last five years remain addiction-free and in stable housing.
engage: Call 503-647-7466 or visit portlandrescuemission.org to learn about New Life Recovery.
content:
Program

A deep, Christ-centered residential recovery journey — counseling and stabilization, job training and employment help, and housing support toward lasting change.

Who It's For

Men and women seeking recovery from addiction and a path out of homelessness.

title: Shepherd's Door (Women & Children Recovery)
description: A long-term residential recovery program for women and mothers with children — a 5-week Discovery stabilization program and a 1-year New Life Recovery program (up to 40 women and 25 children), with trauma-informed childcare, parenting classes, legal/healthcare advocacy, and a dedicated Children's Center.
engage: Submit an intake form at portlandrescuemission.org or call (503) 746-9743. Located at 13207 NE Halsey St, Portland.
content:
Programs

  • Discovery: a 5-week foundational stabilization program with relapse prevention
  • New Life Recovery: a 1-year residential program for up to 40 women and 25 children

Services

Addiction recovery, trauma-informed childcare, parenting classes, legal and healthcare advocacy, life skills, and a Children's Center with after-school and summer programming.

Who It's For

Women and mothers with children seeking recovery.

title: Hooper Detoxification & Stabilization Center
description: Inpatient and outpatient withdrawal management (detox) for people in early recovery from alcohol, opioids, and other drugs, staffed by nurses, physicians, counselors, and recovery mentors. Walk-in admission Mon–Fri 6:45–7:45am; most stays 3–8 days. Accepts OHP and insurance; no one turned away for inability to pay.
engage: Walk in for admission Mon–Fri, 6:45–7:45am, at 1535 N Williams Ave, Portland, or call (503) 238-2067.
content:
Services

  • Inpatient (24/7): withdrawal management and stabilization for alcohol, opioid, and other drug use
  • Outpatient: medical evaluation and medication-supported recovery

Access

Walk-in admission Mon–Fri 6:45–7:45am; admission based on severity of need and capacity. Most stays last 3–8 days. Accepts Oregon Health Plan and private insurance.

title: Old Town Clinic (Health Care for the Homeless)
description: Comprehensive primary care, mental health support, substance use treatment, and an on-site pharmacy for people experiencing homelessness, in Portland's Old Town. Phone and video visits available.
engage: Call the clinic at (503) 228-4533. Located at 727 W Burnside St, Portland. Open Mon–Fri (Wed opens 9am).
content:
Services

  • Primary care for acute and chronic health concerns
  • Mental health support
  • Substance use disorder care
  • On-site pharmacy
  • Phone and video appointments

Who It's For

People experiencing homelessness, with wraparound connections to housing and employment.

title: Puentes (Latino Behavioral Health)
description: Culturally-specific mental health and substance use treatment for the Latino/a/e community and Spanish speakers — individual and group counseling, outpatient addiction treatment, peer support, and case management from bicultural, bilingual clinicians. Accepts OHP.
engage: Call (503) 546-9975 (Mon–Fri 9am–5pm) — bilingual staff assist with intake and scheduling in English and Spanish.
content:
Services

  • Individual and group counseling
  • Outpatient addiction treatment
  • Peer support and case management

Who It's For

Adults 18+ in the Latino/a/e Hispanic community and non-English speakers in Multnomah County and surrounding areas. Bicultural, bilingual (English/Spanish) clinicians.

title: Letty Owings Center (Treatment for Mothers)
description: A 29-bed residential addiction treatment program for pregnant women and mothers of children under 6, with on-site childcare/nursery, prenatal and postpartum care, counseling, and parenting support — helping mothers stay with their children. Accepts OHP, Medicare, and self-pay. (Waitlist currently paused — check availability.)
engage: Call the intake coordinator at (971) 352-8740 or front desk (971) 352-8765, or email LettyOwingsIntake@ccconcern.org. Note: admissions may be temporarily paused.
content:
Program

29-bed residential treatment for pregnant women and mothers of children under 6 with substance use disorders — many of whom would otherwise face child removal.

Services

  • Residential treatment with on-site nursery/childcare
  • Individual and group counseling
  • Prenatal and postpartum health care
  • Parenting skills workshops and case management

title: Blackburn Center (Integrated Health & Housing)
description: An integrated center combining primary care, mental health, addiction treatment, an on-site pharmacy, employment help, and housing for 100+ residents (mostly in recovery) — all under one roof in East Portland.
engage: Call the clinic at (971) 361-7700 (pharmacy 971-361-7800). Located at 12121 E Burnside St, Portland.
content:
Services (all under one roof)

  • Primary care and routine health
  • Mental health support
  • Addiction / substance use treatment
  • On-site pharmacy
  • Employment assistance
  • Housing for 100+ residents, mostly in recovery

Who It's For

People experiencing homelessness and struggling with addiction or mental health challenges.

title: Supportive Housing & Employment
description: A continuum of affordable and supportive housing integrated with social services, plus job training and placement through Central City Staffing — helping people experiencing homelessness find home, regain health, and reach long-term stability. Serving Portland since 1979.
engage: Learn about housing and employment programs at centralcityconcern.org, or connect through any Central City Concern clinic or recovery program.
content:
Services

  • Comprehensive continuum of affordable and supportive housing
  • Job training and placement (Central City Staffing social enterprise)
  • Integrated with health care and recovery services

Who It's For

People experiencing or at risk of homelessness — about 14,400 served annually.

title: Home Base Recovery (Men's Treatment & Housing)
description: A culturally-responsive treatment program helping men 18+ make positive life changes — substance use treatment, problem-gambling support, mental health services, naturopathic care, counseling, and mentorship with housing. No one denied for inability to pay (sliding scale).
engage: Contact a lead mentor for an intake screening: (971) 806-2243 (jquintero@voaor.org) or (971) 334-2004 (dspotanski@voaor.org). General: (503) 228-9229.
content:
Services

  • Substance abuse treatment
  • Problem gambling support
  • Mental health services and counseling
  • Naturopathic medical care
  • Mentorship with housing

Who It's For

Men ages 18+. Culturally-responsive approach. No one denied services for inability to pay; sliding fee available.
centerings: Men; People in Recovery; Low Barrier

title: Men's Residential Center (MRC)
description: A residential addiction treatment center for men in SE Portland, providing structured, live-in recovery support.
engage: Call (503) 235-8655 to ask about the Men's Residential Center. Located at 3910 SE Stark St, Portland.
content:
Program

Residential (live-in) substance use disorder treatment for men, with structured recovery programming and support.

Who It's For

Men seeking residential addiction treatment.

title: Veteran Shelters & Supportive Housing
description: Low-barrier shelter for veterans experiencing homelessness (including the Thayer Family Foundation Veterans Shelter and Veteran's Village) plus veteran-specific permanent supportive housing, most through HUD-VASH vouchers.
engage: Submit a referral at dogoodmultnomah.org/refer. For HUD-VASH housing screening, contact the VA's Community Resource & Referral Center at 503-808-1256 or VHAPOR-CRSOutreach@va.gov. Waitlists apply.
content:
Services

  • Low-barrier veteran shelters (Thayer Family Foundation Veterans Shelter, Veteran's Village, and others)
  • Veteran-specific permanent supportive housing (mostly HUD-VASH)
  • Case management and support

Who It's For

Veterans experiencing homelessness. Waitlists apply due to demand.

title: Alternative & Village Shelters
description: Alternative shelter options operated by Do Good Multnomah, including St. Johns Village and Kiggins Village, serving people experiencing homelessness. (Civilian beds often full — check current availability.)
engage: Submit a referral at dogoodmultnomah.org/refer or check dogoodmultnomah.org/alternative-shelter for current openings. Waitlists apply.
content:
Shelters

Village-model and alternative shelters including St. Johns Village and Kiggins Village.

Who It's For

People experiencing homelessness. Civilian beds are often at capacity with waitlists — check back for availability.

title: SNAP Food Benefits
description: Monthly food benefits (SNAP) to help low-income Oregonians buy groceries, loaded onto an EBT card. Apply online, by phone, or in person.
engage: Apply at one.oregon.gov or call 800-699-9075 (Mon–Fri 7am–6pm). In-person help at local ODHS offices statewide.
content:
What It Provides

Monthly SNAP benefits to help buy food, based on household income and size.

How to Apply

Online at one.oregon.gov, by phone (800-699-9075), or at a local ODHS office.

title: Oregon Health Plan (OHP)
description: Oregon's Medicaid program providing free or low-cost medical, dental, and behavioral health coverage to eligible low-income Oregonians, plus help with long-term care and Medicare costs.
engage: Apply at one.oregon.gov or call 800-699-9075. Coverage is free for those who qualify.
content:
What It Provides

Medical, dental, and behavioral health coverage (Oregon's Medicaid), plus long-term care assistance and Medicare cost support for eligible people.

How to Apply

Online at one.oregon.gov, by phone, or at a local office.

title: TANF Cash Assistance
description: Temporary cash assistance (TANF) for low-income families with children and new parents, to help with basic needs while working toward stability.
engage: Apply at one.oregon.gov or call 800-699-9075.
content:
What It Provides

Monthly cash assistance for low-income families with children and new parents.

Who's Eligible

Low-income families with dependent children.

title: ERDC Child Care Assistance
description: Employment-Related Day Care (ERDC) helps working families and student parents afford child care so they can keep working or studying.
engage: Apply at one.oregon.gov or call 800-699-9075.
content:
What It Provides

Help paying for child care for working families and students, based on income and family size.

Who's Eligible

Working or studying low-income families with children.

title: TA-DVS (Domestic Violence Assistance)
description: Temporary Assistance for Domestic Violence Survivors (TA-DVS) provides short-term help — including housing and moving-cost assistance — for families experiencing domestic violence.
engage: Apply at one.oregon.gov or call 800-699-9075. Local ODHS offices can help.
content:
What It Provides

Short-term financial help for families experiencing domestic violence, including housing and moving costs, to help establish safety.

Who's Eligible

Families experiencing domestic violence.

title: Emergency & Navigation Shelters
description: A network of 24/7 low-barrier emergency shelters across Portland for men, women, and couples — including Banfield (medically vulnerable), Laurelwood, River District Navigation Center, Walnut Park, and Willamette Center. Access by reservation/referral, not drop-in.
engage: Request a bed with the shelter access pre-screening form at tprojects.org/shelter-access-pre-screening-form, or visit the Resource Center at 650 NW Irving St, Portland. Info: 503-280-4700.
content:
Shelters (24/7 low-barrier)

  • Banfield Shelter Motel — medically vulnerable men, women, couples (60 beds)
  • Laurelwood Center — women and couples (120 beds)
  • River District Navigation Center — men, women, couples (90 beds)
  • Walnut Park — men, women, couples (72 beds)
  • Willamette Center — women and couples (130 beds)

Access

By reservation or referral (not drop-in) via the online pre-screening form or the Resource Center.

title: SE Grand Recovery Shelter (Alcohol- & Drug-Free)
description: An overnight shelter with 140 beds (expandable to 200 in severe weather) for people seeking an alcohol- and drug-free environment to support their recovery.
engage: Request a bed via the pre-screening form at tprojects.org/shelter-access-pre-screening-form or the Resource Center (650 NW Irving St). Info: 503-280-4700.
content:
Shelter

A 140-bed overnight recovery shelter (up to 200 beds in severe weather) providing a substance-free environment for people in or seeking recovery.

title: Short-Term Residential Shelters & Resource Center
description: Short-term residential shelters — Clark Center (men, some justice-involved), Doreen's Place (men), and Jean's Place (women, some justice-involved and veterans) — plus the Resource Center entry point for shelter access and housing/reunification services.
engage: Start at the Resource Center, 650 NW Irving St, Portland, or complete the pre-screening form online. Admin: 503-280-4700 (Mon–Fri 9am–5pm).
content:
Short-Term Residential Shelters

  • Clark Center — men (90 beds; some for community justice participants)
  • Doreen's Place — men (90 beds)
  • Jean's Place — women (60 beds; some for justice-involved residents and veterans)

Resource Center

The entry point for shelter access and housing/reunification services, at 650 NW Irving St.
